identity.title: Still Got 5 On It
identity.styleSentence: A grimy East Coast boom bap track at 92 BPM featuring dusty drums with heavy swing and hard snare cracks over thick vinyl hiss, anchored by a foghorn sub-bass and sinister minor-key piano; the production lifts with a bright xylophone melody doubled on mellow flute that weaves through the hook to answer raw confessional vocals from a raspy male lead rapper supported by gruff call-and-response ad-libs, while a smooth smoky R&B chorus delivers passionate vibrato in layered harmonies against muted trumpet shadows and eerie glassy synth motifs.
identity.vocalStyle: raspy male lead with raw confessional tone alternating with gruff call-and-response ad-libs, contrasted by a smooth smoky R&B chorus featuring passionate vibrato and layered harmonies

lyrics.text:
[Intro — Projector Hiss, Distant Whistle]

[Low Male Voice]
Yeah...

Still got five on it.

[Second Voice]
Five?

[Low Male Voice]
Man, five used to mean somethin'.

[Beat Drops]

[Hook — Smoky Male R&B, Simple]

Still got five on it
Still got five on it
But five don't buy what five did

Still got five on it
Still got five on it
Man, tell me where the good days went

[Verse 1 — Rooklyn]

Five in the hand, that was enough back then
Little bag came through, everybody got in
One had the papers, one had the light
One little five kept the room good all night

Windows cracked, beat played low
Good herb hit before the first damn smoke
Sticky little fingers when you broke that green
You knew what it was, you knew what I mean

[Second Voice]
No label?

[Rooklyn]
Nah.

[Second Voice]
No fancy name?

[Rooklyn]
Nah.

Just good clean smoke and a pocket of change
Now five hit the table, everybody look strange

I said, “Lemme get five.”

He said—

[Beat Cuts]

“Five of what?”

[Hook — Bigger]

Still got five on it
Still got five on it
But five don't buy what five did

Still got five on it
Still got five on it
Man, tell me where the good days went

[Chorus — Layered, Sing-Along]

Five used to stretch
Five used to last
Five had the whole damn room on blast

Five used to stretch
Five used to live
Now five don't buy what five used to give

Still got five on it
Still got five on it
But five don't buy what five did

[Verse 2 — Rooklyn]

Now the price went up and the trust went down
Too much mystery smoke going round this town
What they spray on that?
What they mix in this?

[Second Voice]
Why it smell like candy and chemical mist?

[Rooklyn]
Nah, keep that bag
I remember them days
Biggest damn problem was finding a place
Somebody got hungry, somebody got loud
Somebody told stories and forgot half out

Now every little strain got a fourteen-word name
“Supernova Nuclear Purple Cocaine”

[Second Voice]
What?

[Rooklyn]
Exactly.

I don't need a barcode
I don't need a brand
I need five dollars' worth
Like five used to understand

[Hook — R&B]

Still got five on it
Still got five on it
But five don't buy what five did

Still got five on it
Still got five on it
Man, tell me where the good days went

[Bridge — Beat Strips Down]

[Male R&B Voice]
Good old days...
Pass it around

Good clean smoke...
Music turned down

Five in the middle...
Everybody chipped in

Now I put five down...

[Pause]

[Rooklyn]
...and they tell me, “Try again.”

[Beat Slams Back]

[Final Chorus — Huge but Grim]

Five used to stretch
Five used to last
Five had the whole damn room on blast

Five used to stretch
Five used to live
Now five don't buy what five used to give

Still got five on it
Still got five on it
But five don't buy what five did

Still got five on it
Still got five on it
Man, tell me where the good days went

[Final Hook — Repeating]

Still got five on it
(Still got five)

Still got five on it
(Still got five)

But five don't buy what five did

Still got five on it
(Still got five)

Still got five on it

[Rooklyn — Spoken]
...Might as well buy chips.

[Second Voice]
Small bag.

[Rooklyn]
Man, fuck 2026.

[Projector Click]

[End]

analysis.summary: The song is a gritty reflection on how inflation and changing markets have devalued money ('five') while destroying the community bonds and quality found in 'the good old days' of unbranded, shared experiences. It captures a melancholic nostalgia for an era where value was defined by trust and camaraderie rather than corporate branding or rising prices.

sections.name: Intro
sections.emotion: Confused longing and weary resignation as the narrator questions where value has gone.
sections.imageryPrompt: Low-angle shot of a dimly lit alleyway with steam rising from manhole covers reflecting neon signs that are flickering out.

sections.name: Verse 1
sections.emotion: Raw storytelling and bittersweet memory, contrasting past unity with current isolation.
sections.imageryPrompt: Close-up of weathered hands holding a small crumpled bill against the backdrop of cracked windowpanes and dust motes dancing in slanted light.

sections.name: Chorus
sections.emotion: Aggressive lamentation, repeating the core truth that money no longer holds its spiritual or communal weight.
sections.imageryPrompt: Wide shot of a rainy city street at midnight where puddles reflect not faces but generic barcodes and chemical runoff.

sections.name: Bridge
sections.emotion: Urgent plea for return to shared values and simple, honest connection.
sections.imageryPrompt: Silhouette of friends sitting in a semi-circle around a small table under the glow of a single streetlamp, passing an object back and forth.

sections.name: Drop
sections.emotion: Defeated acceptance and cynical dismissal of repeated attempts to fix what is broken.
sections.imageryPrompt: Abstract visual of a heavy stone door slamming shut in slow motion with dust particles freezing in the air.

sections.name: Verse 2
sections.emotion: Resigned observation of value eroding over time, accepting that 'five' is now dead.
sections.imageryPrompt: Time-lapse style shot showing a vibrant neighborhood slowly turning gray and silent as the colors drain from the buildings.

keywords.imageryPrompt: A small crumpled dollar bill floating in a void, its edges burning away into gray ash.
Thick fog rolling off dark water where shadowy figures stand motionless under flickering sodium lights.
Bright artificial colors oozing from a cracked pipe, contrasting sharply with the dull grime around it.
A giant corporate logo stamped over a faceless crowd of people walking in unison like robots.
Two hands reaching out to shake but fading into smoke before they make contact, leaving only empty air.
Viscous purple liquid dripping from a ceiling onto a rusty floor, bubbling with unnatural light.
